text: The LORD sent Nathan to David, and Nathan came and told him about two men who lived in the same city, one rich and one poor.
The rich man owned very large flocks and herds.

The poor man had nothing at all except one little ewe lamb he had bought and raised; it grew up alongside him and his children, eating his food, drinking from his cup, and sleeping in his arms like a daughter.
When a traveler came to visit the rich man, he refused to take anything from his own flocks or herds to feed his guest, and instead took the poor man's lamb and served it to the visitor.
David was furious at the man and told Nathan that whoever did such a thing deserved to die.
He said the man must pay back four times over for the lamb, because he had shown no pity.
Nathan said to David, you are that man; the LORD, the God of Israel, had anointed him king over Israel and saved him from Saul's hand.
God had given him his master's house and wives and the whole house of Israel and Judah, and would have given him much more besides, if that had not been enough.
So why had David despised the LORD's command by doing this evil — striking down Uriah with the sword and taking his wife, using the Ammonites' sword to kill him?
Because of this, the sword would never leave David's household, since he had despised the LORD and taken Uriah's wife for himself.
The LORD said he would bring trouble against David out of his own household, and would take his wives before his eyes and give them to someone close to him, who would lie with them in broad daylight.
David had done his deed in secret, but God would do this in front of all Israel, in plain daylight.
David said to Nathan, I have sinned against the LORD. Nathan replied that the LORD had taken away his sin, and he would not die for it.
But because this act had given the LORD's enemies good reason to mock him, the son born to David would die.
After Nathan went home, the LORD struck the child that Bathsheba had borne David, and it became gravely ill.
David pleaded with God for the boy's life; he fasted and spent the nights lying on the ground.
The elders of his household tried to get him to rise, but he refused and would not eat anything with them.
On the seventh day the child died, and David's servants were afraid to tell him, worried about how he might react given his state while the boy was still alive.
When David noticed his servants whispering, he realized the child had died, and they confirmed it when he asked.
So David got up from the ground, washed, put on fresh clothes and lotion, went to worship at the house of the LORD, then returned home, asked for food, and ate.
His servants asked why he was acting this way, fasting and weeping while the child lived but rising to eat once it had died.
David explained that while the child was alive he had fasted and wept, hoping the LORD might show mercy and let the boy live.
But now that he was dead, there was no reason to fast — he could not bring the child back, but one day he would go to be with him.
David comforted his wife Bathsheba, went to her, and she gave birth to a son, whom he named Solomon; the LORD loved the boy.
Through Nathan the prophet, the LORD sent word giving the child the name Jedidiah, because of the LORD's love for him.
Meanwhile Joab kept fighting against Rabbah of the Ammonites and captured the royal city's water supply.
He sent messengers to David to report that he had attacked Rabbah and taken its water supply.
He urged David to gather the rest of the troops, camp against the city, and capture it himself, so that Joab would not be the one credited with its fall.
So David gathered all the remaining troops, marched to Rabbah, attacked it, and captured it.
He took the crown off their king's head — a heavy weight of gold set with a precious stone — and placed it on his own head, and carried off a great amount of plunder from the city.

He brought out the people of the city and put them to forced labor with saws, iron picks, and axes, and set them to work making bricks; he did this to all the Ammonite cities. Then David and the whole army returned to Jerusalem.
